title: ShowerBoost
summary: AI-assisted product concept prototype — a portable, battery-powered shower pressure booster pitched as an investor-ready landing page using Lovable and prompt-driven design.
overview: Used Lovable (AI-assisted) to rapidly prototype a product concept landing page for ShowerBoost — a portable, rechargeable shower pressure booster that screws onto any existing shower fitting.
problem: Millions of renters, apartment dwellers, and travellers endure weak shower pressure with no practical fix. Whole-house pumps require permanent installation; high-pressure heads barely help. There is no portable, renter-friendly solution on the market.
process: Iterative prompt-driven design across six cycles: hero and value proposition, problem framing, product concept and features, comparison table, investor-facing 'why doesn't this exist?' section, and feedback capture.
outcomes: A credible, investor-ready prototype site built in hours. Narrative structure (problem → solution → why now) proved more persuasive than visual polish alone. AI tools compressed concept-to-prototype timeline dramatically.

title: GoPro App Redesign
summary: making gopro fun and easy for all—a faster path from capture to share with ‘Magic Movie’.
overview: A conceptual project focused on improving the GoPro app's user experience, from initial device pairing to sharing final edits.
problem: The existing app was perceived as complex, with a steep learning curve for new users and inefficient media management for power users.
process: The process involved user research, competitive analysis, persona creation, user flow mapping, wireframing, and high-fidelity prototyping.
outcomes: The redesigned app features a streamlined onboarding process, intuitive media gallery with smart sorting, and a simplified video editor.

title: Wellness features in food delivery apps — heat-safe riding in a warming city
summary: Humanitech brief: empower communities via technology in the face of climate change. We focused on food delivery riders working in extreme heat and prototyped wellness features inside an existing rider app. Research → concepts → mid-fi → client feedback → hi-fi + testing, with clear learnings and next steps.
overview: Humanitech’s brief (Australian Red Cross): Define what it means to empower a community through technology; identify themes worth exploring; map lived experience; develop concepts; and prototype solutions that prioritise psychosocial wellbeing and livelihoods.
problem: Climate change is increasing the frequency and intensity of heatwaves in Australian cities. Riders working outdoors are vulnerable; safety features are fragmented or absent in their day-to-day tools.
process: We began broad (heat risk & public-health literature), then narrowed to delivery riders as a high-exposure group we could reach quickly for research. We used a mixed-methods approach including desk research, field interviews in Adelaide/Perth/Hong Kong, stakeholder review, affinity mapping, problem scale, personas, journey mapping, ideation with client, MVP matrix, detailed & simple user flows, and mid-fi/hi-fi prototyping with usability testing rounds.
outcomes: The final concept integrated wellness features into the rider app (starting with Deliveroo), including map markers for water, shade, sunscreen, and daily check-ins to earn credits redeemable at partner vendors, and quick tips on heat stress. Iterative testing led to significant improvements in usability and clarity.
role: Team lead / client contact, UX researcher, UI designer, co-presenter

title: Bestie Health Club – MVP Discovery
summary: Discovery and strategy for a minimum viable product for a new pet health app.
overview: Worked with a startup to define the core features and user experience for their MVP, focusing on community and accountability.
problem: The startup had a broad vision but needed to narrow down the scope to an achievable MVP that would attract early adopters.
process: Facilitated workshops, conducted market research, and created a feature-prioritisation matrix to define the MVP scope.
outcomes: A clear, documented MVP strategy, user stories, and initial wireframes for the key user flows.

title: Trip Approve – Onboarding
summary: Designing a seamless onboarding experience for a B2B travel approval platform.
overview: Improved the activation rate for new corporate clients by redesigning the user and administrator onboarding flows.
problem: New users were dropping off during setup due to a confusing and lengthy onboarding process.
process: Analysed user data, conducted usability tests on the existing flow, and designed an interactive, step-by-step onboarding guide.
outcomes: Increased user activation by 30% and reduced support tickets related to setup by 50%.

title: UX Survey of Learning Design Practice — LEI & TIU 2025
summary: A quick pulse on practices, confidence, barriers, and platform preferences among learning design practitioners.
overview: A standalone survey case study covering objectives, method, results and implications for future ux-for-learning initiatives.
problem: A pulse was needed to inform UX capability support and platform choices for learning designers.
process: A live poll was conducted using Mentimeter during a division meeting, followed by an identical Jotform survey for asynchronous participation.
outcomes: The survey identified key barriers (time constraints), support preferences (workshops, exemplars), and a strong preference for Canvas as the platform for future resources.
team: Rich Bartlett, Kelli Knuth, Tim Churchward, Alex Price

title: UX Group - User Testing Four Designs
summary: Learning From Students: Usability testing the new Adelaide University course home page. We tested four designs (current, numbered variant, Dynamic Home, and course info patterns) with 42 students. One-on-one testing proved most valuable; results are now shaping the template and navigation.
overview: This project involved organising and leading a user testing workshop with a faculty UX interest group to gather feedback on four different design mockups for a new online module.
problem: The development team was at an impasse, with strong opinions supporting four different design directions. An unbiased, data-driven approach was needed to make a decision.
process: I prepared testing scripts and prototypes, facilitated the testing sessions, synthesised the qualitative and quantitative feedback, and presented a final report with clear recommendations.
outcomes: The user testing provided clear evidence for which design was most effective and why. The chosen design was implemented with minor modifications based on the feedback, leading to a successful launch.

title: “Flock” Hackathon
summary: A 48-hour hackathon project to build a social event coordination app.
overview: Developed a functional prototype for an app that simplifies organising casual meetups with friends.
problem: Organising group events often involves messy group chats and difficulty in tracking RSVPs and logistics.
process: Rapid ideation, feature prioritisation, parallel design and development, and a final presentation.
outcomes: Won the 'Best User Experience' award. The prototype featured poll-based decision making and a centralised event dashboard.

title: Rock, Paper, Scissors - online battle tournament
summary: AI-assisted build with Firebase Studio for a 50+ person meeting.
overview: Began as a novice to Firebase; used AI guidance to scaffold, commit, and iterate. Goal: a fast, inclusive rock–paper–scissors tournament for our division’s monthly meeting.
problem: Run a lightweight tournament many can follow on one call, minimal setup. Handle ties and bracket progression; clearly show who plays next. Keep interaction simple so pod managers can represent teams without confusion.
process: Used Firebase Studio to scaffold quickly; iterated on arena styling, bracket, and “latest result”. Paired with an engineer to stabilise real-time reads/writes and repair regressions from rapid AI-led edits.
outcomes: A working multiplayer tournament game deployed on Firebase Hosting. Facilitation model tested and ready for a live 50+ person event.

title: UX Design Toolkit (TUX) for Learning Design
summary: A UX toolkit providing reusable components, templates, and guidelines to ensure consistency and quality across online courses.
overview: This project involves creating a comprehensive UX Design Toolkit specifically tailored for learning designers. The toolkit, codenamed TUX, provides reusable components, templates, and guidelines to ensure consistency and quality across all online courses.
problem: Learning designers often have to reinvent the wheel for each new course, leading to inconsistencies in user experience and inefficient workflows. There is a need for a centralised, standardised set of tools.
process: The process includes auditing existing courses, identifying common UI patterns, developing a component library in Figma, and creating documentation on usage and best practices. The toolkit is being developed with AI-assisted tools and will be available via a GitHub repository.
outcomes: The expected outcome is a robust design system that will accelerate the course development process, improve the student user experience, and foster a more collaborative design culture.

title: Canvas LMS Quick Navigation Bar
summary: Reducing clicks with an accessible, reusable on-page nav for modules and topics.
overview: A lightweight, consistent quick navigation element embedded on Canvas pages to jump between topics, assignments and key resources with fewer clicks.
problem: Frequent navigation requires multiple clicks and context switching. Patterns vary across courses, increasing cognitive load. Designers often lack theme access, restricting global changes.
process: Reviewed current course pages, prototyped HTML/CSS in a test shell, confirmed accessibility, and created variants. Consulted with the media team for alignment with global navigation plans.
outcomes: A consistent in-page pattern ready for wider trial, faster setup for designers, positive pilot feedback, and alignment with the media team.

title: When not to code: why simple tools sometimes work best
summary: A short exploration of in-class vibe-checks using Mentimeter, a bespoke Firebase Studio app, and a Miro board, highlighting why existing tools can be the best choice.
overview:
A short, fast exploration of ways to run an in-class vibe/mental-health check-in. I mocked up options in Mentimeter (using its AI tool), built a bespoke prototype in Firebase Studio (“VibeCheck”), and tested a first-time Miro approach created by Dr Elysia Sokolenko.
Headline insight: where an existing platform already fits the workflow (Canvas + Miro), building a custom app can be unnecessary overhead.
problem: Academics asked for a quick way to sense how a cohort was feeling during workshops that was low friction, visually interpretable, and easy to reuse in Canvas.
process: Explored Mentimeter's AI, built a bespoke prototype in Firebase Studio, and tested a first-time Miro approach. Compared the options against the core need.
outcomes: The Miro board approach was adopted as a template. The key insight was that for this context, an existing platform that fit the workflow was superior to a custom app, avoiding unnecessary engineering overhead.
team: Dr Elysia Sokolenko, Dr Bonnie Williams, Anna Leonard

title: UoA Online: Learning Design at Scale
summary: Lead Learning Designer for eight online university courses across the Bachelor of International Business and Health Services Management programmes at the University of Adelaide.
overview: Created a scalable framework for designing high-quality online learning experiences for Open Universities Australia (OUA).
problem: The lack of a standardised process led to inconsistent course quality and inefficient use of resources.
process: Researched learning design models, consulted with stakeholders, and created a multi-stage design and development guide.
outcomes: The new process was adopted across multiple faculties, improving baseline course quality and reducing development time.

title: Communication Styles Quiz
summary: A static PDF activity was transformed into an accessible, embeddable web quiz for the Create. Connect. Communicate. course. Built in collaboration with Media, the tool adds clear micro-interactions, printable results, and a streamlined single-choice flow that works standalone or inside Canvas.
overview: Converted a static “Communication Styles” PDF worksheet into a lightweight, mobile-friendly interactive quiz to guide learners through 23 quick selections, provide immediate feedback, and produce a printable summary.
problem: The original PDF worksheet was not engaging, difficult to use on mobile devices, and did not provide instant feedback to the learners.
process: Collaborated with the Media team to resolve conflicts in the source PDF, verify the live build and print-to-PDF flow, and implement quick UX wins like improved copy, subtle animations, and clearer controls, while keeping the core single-question-per-screen flow.
outcomes: Replaced a multi-page PDF with a focused interactive that’s quicker to complete, easier to embed, and produces a clean PDF record. This created a repeatable pattern for future PDF-to-interactive conversions.
role: Product/UX, copy review, academic liaison, QA
team: Rich Bartlett, Hui Mu (Media), Course Academics

title: Redesigning Course Orientation (OUA)
summary: A ground-up rethink of the OUA orientation sequence: evaluate the current experience, co-design with students, prototype in Figma, and iterate from real feedback. Outcomes: clearer home/orientation hub, streamlined navigation, stronger wayfinding (cue boxes), and an implementation blueprint for the media/LMS teams.
overview: When I joined the Online Programs Team, one of my first tasks was to re-imagine orientation for OUA students. Guided by my manager Tim and working closely with my colleague Sinead, we audited existing orientation patterns across courses, then shaped a version that fits OUA learners: informative, engaging, and easy to navigate online.
problem: Existing orientation sequences were inconsistent and often failed to provide a clear, engaging start for online students, leading to confusion and disengagement.
process: The project followed a phased approach: Evaluation of the current state, co-design with a student focus group, development and facilitation of a Miro-based workshop, and synthesis of feedback into actionable Figma prototypes.
outcomes: The redesigned orientation resulted in a clearer home/orientation hub, streamlined navigation, and stronger wayfinding. The project delivered a detailed implementation blueprint for the media and LMS teams, grounded in direct student feedback.

title: Personal Professional Development — Course Design (ENTREP1002)
summary: A full design and build of ENTREP1002 Personal Professional Development: aligning outcomes, assessments and weekly learning to help students form a professional identity and portfolio. Deliverables included course alignment, personas and narrative, UX copy, assessment exemplars, and data visualisation upgrades. Collaboration across academics, learning designers and media resulted in a coherent, visual, week-by-week student experience.
overview: The Personal Professional Development course is a structured educational offering aimed at equipping students with a comprehensive set of professional skills and an understanding of professional identity in the business world.
problem: The primary challenge was to translate a complex set of academic and professional development concepts into a coherent, engaging, and practical 12-week online course for university students.
process: The process involved collaborative brainstorming with academics, iterative design of the course structure, development of student personas to guide narrative, and creation of visual and interactive learning artefacts.
outcomes: The final result was a fully-developed online course with a clear narrative, structured assessments, and a rich set of learning resources designed to foster student engagement and professional growth.
team: Course Authors, Learning Designers, Media Team

title: Expandable References for Better UX
summary: Enhanced a course page by integrating an expandable 'show references' feature, applying the principle of progressive disclosure to simplify the UI.
overview: In this project, I enhanced a course page by integrating an expandable 'show references' feature. This design aligns with the principle of progressive disclosure, which defers advanced or less frequently used features to secondary screens, simplifying the user interface and reducing cognitive load.
problem: The original page presented a lengthy reference list, requiring extensive scrolling and potentially overwhelming students. This layout hindered efficient navigation and detracted from the primary content.
process: Implemented an expandable 'show references' box. This feature allows students to access references on demand, providing greater control and freedom to navigate content according to their needs. The references section is initially collapsed, displaying a link to expand. When clicked, the full list of references expands below, allowing students to view them without leaving the current page.
outcomes: The integration of the expandable 'show references' feature resulted in a cleaner, more intuitive interface. Students can now focus on the main content without unnecessary distractions, and access references seamlessly when needed. This project underscores the importance of user-centred design in educational materials.

title: H5P Student Handbook Conversion
summary: From a 49-page PDF to a clear, 10-page interactive handbook
overview: Academics Cristian and Han needed their 49-page PDF handbook transformed into a modern, navigable, student-friendly digital version. I designed and built an H5P framework that removed tutor-only content, reduced cognitive load, and re-structured information into accessible, interactive components.
problem: The original handbook existed only as a 49-page PDF. Students had to hunt through dense pages, and tutor-only guidance appeared alongside student information—adding confusion and unnecessary cognitive load. The request: convert the handbook to a streamlined H5P experience that modernises presentation, improves navigation, and removes extraneous content.
process: I created the initial H5P framework in my sandpit and began transferring content from the PDF. Cristian annotated the PDF with clear red mark-ups indicating what could be safely removed, which guided the scope of the build. After the prototype was ready, I engaged the Casual Course Builders in the Media Team to help complete content import and implement Cristian’s final change requests.
outcomes: The new interactive H5P handbook reduced navigation time by consolidating content and adding clear wayfinding, lowered cognitive load, and provided better maintainability with structured, reusable H5P content patterns for future updates.
role: UX lead, content architect, H5P builder
team: Cristian (academic), Han (academic), Casual Course Builders (Media Team)

title: Everyday tools for the online Learning Designer
summary: A practical intro talk linking UX methods to online Learning Design (LX). We showed how to map & sequence content, design interactions, use H5P, and apply usability, schema theory and cognitive load. Attendance was low (torrential rain + light promo), which became a key lesson—great content still needs great distribution.
overview: A fast, tool-rich session for designers and educators showing how everyday UX practices translate directly into online learning design (LX).
problem: Designers and educators often struggle to see the direct application of UX principles in their day-to-day learning design work.
process: Co-presented a workshop that demonstrated practical translations of UX to LX, including information architecture, HCI, interaction design, usability, and learning science principles, using everyday tools like Canvas, H5P, and Miro.
outcomes: The session provided a clear framework for applying UX to LX. Despite low attendance due to external factors, the content itself was well-received and highlighted the need for better promotion strategies for valuable content.

title: Publications
summary: A collection of my published articles, papers, and other works.
overview: This section provides links to my published work, including articles, conference papers, and other scholarly contributions.

title: AU Course Development: Part 3
summary: Supporting Academic Course Developers at Adelaide University through structured 10-week design and build cycles, aligned to the Adelaide Attainment Model.
overview: As a Learning Designer embedded in the Teaching & Learning Innovation unit, I supported Academic Course Developers across multiple 10-week development cycles to design, build, and deliver first and second year courses for the new Adelaide University curriculum — underpinned by the Adelaide Attainment Model.
problem: A brand-new university curriculum required courses to be built at scale within tight 10-week cycles, with academic staff needing structured support to achieve constructive alignment, quality Canvas builds, and handover-ready courses.
process: Worked across multiple cycles from 2024 onwards, supporting academics through the Course Details Form, constructive alignment of CLOs, assessment design, Canvas builds, H5P activities, media briefs, and course readiness handovers.
outcomes: Multiple courses completed across disciplines including STEM, Health Sciences, Business, and AI/Computing — each meeting AU's Course Completion and Teaching Readiness criteria.
team: Teaching & Learning Innovation unit (LDs, CEDs, Digital Education Developers, Media team)
